coredata.dc:title: Rare-earth free solid-state fluorescent carbon-quantum dots: Multi-color emission and its application as optical dual-mode sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Latief, Urosa
Islam, Shafi Ul
Khan, Mohd. Shahid
coredata.dc:description: Proposing a straightforward technique for producing multi-color carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and investigating how luminescence can be tuned and improved is appealing and encouraging. The production of white light-emitting nanophosphors is crucial for the development of future display and lighting technologies, and their outstanding luminescent qualities provide considerable potential in sensing applications. The rare-earth ions have been used often as dopants to produce semiconducting nanophosphors, the restricted unavailability of rare-earth elements in our planet necessitates to look for more appropriate substitutes. Herein, we present the fabrication of CQDs co-doped with Cu and Mn (Cu/Mn:CQDs) transition metal ions that exhibit strong white light emission with the Commission Internationale de L′Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates (0.36, 0.30), which has a high color rendering index (CRI) value of 89. The as-produced Cu/Mn:CQDs have a quantum yield of 28.35 % and are employed as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA) detection through "on-off-on" sequential mode. The fluorescence of Cu/Mn:CQDs can be greatly suppressed by Fe3+ ions via static quenching and then recovered by adding AA, as AA causes Fe3+ to be reduced to Fe2+. This sensor demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ and AA with detection limits of 16 and 46 nM, respectively. Most importantly, the as-proposed fluorescence sensing system are successful for detecting Fe3+ and AA in real water samples with satisfactory results. Furthermore, an IMPLICATION logic gate is built based on the unique properties of Cu/Mn:CQDs nanohybrids, indicating new prospects for application in single and multiple biological sensing. This research could open the path for the development of low cost, high performance and environment-friendly multicolor phosphor for optoelectronic devices and sensing applications.

head.ce:title: Rare-earth free solid-state fluorescent carbon-quantum dots: Multi-color emission and its application as optical dual-mode sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi 110025, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Jamia Millia Islamia

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi 110025, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Proposing a straightforward technique for producing multi-color carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and investigating how luminescence can be tuned and improved is appealing and encouraging. The production of white light-emitting nanophosphors is crucial for the development of future display and lighting technologies, and their outstanding luminescent qualities provide considerable potential in sensing applications. The rare-earth ions have been used often as dopants to produce semiconducting nanophosphors, the restricted unavailability of rare-earth elements in our planet necessitates to look for more appropriate substitutes. Herein, we present the fabrication of CQDs co-doped with Cu and Mn (Cu/Mn:CQDs) transition metal ions that exhibit strong white light emission with the Commission Internationale de L′Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates (0.36, 0.30), which has a high color rendering index (CRI) value of 89. The as-produced Cu/Mn:CQDs have a quantum yield of 28.35 % and are employed as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA) detection through "on-off-on" sequential mode. The fluorescence of Cu/Mn:CQDs can be greatly suppressed by Fe3+ ions via static quenching and then recovered by adding AA, as AA causes Fe3+ to be reduced to Fe2+. This sensor demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity towards Fe3+ and AA with detection limits of 16 and 46 nM, respectively. Most importantly, the as-proposed fluorescence sensing system are successful for detecting Fe3+ and AA in real water samples with satisfactory results. Furthermore, an IMPLICATION logic gate is built based on the unique properties of Cu/Mn:CQDs nanohybrids, indicating new prospects for application in single and multiple biological sensing. This research could open the path for the development of low cost, high performance and environment-friendly multicolor phosphor for optoelectronic devices and sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a brand-new class of carbon nanomaterial, have drawn scientist’s growing attention. Comparatively speaking to organic dyes and traditional semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs inherit biocompatibility and low toxicity in addition to their high fluorescence, high chemical stability, exceptional water solubility, and useful surface functionalization capabilities [1,2]. In addition, CQDs have shown an unmatched value for applications in a variety of fields, including sensing, imaging, and light-emitting diodes [3–7]. However, CQDs currently have a few problems. Since the majority of CQDs documented in the literature are blue-fluorescent, which considerably restricts their biological applicability [8,9]. Alternatively, long wavelength fluorescence emission of CQDs are referred to as favorable due to their applications, particularly in the biomedical fields, which have the ability to provide the benefits that can be greatly reaped commercially [10,11]. Additionally, the fluorescence of solid CQDs is often muted as a result of aggregation, restricting its use in light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [12–14]. It is hence highly challenging to obtain CQDs in an aqueous solution, with long wavelength emission. Solid-state light sources known as "LEDs" are able to transform electrical power directly into light. It has a variety of advantages, including small size, improved luminescence, long life, and low power consumption [15,16]. In the interim, white LEDs have seen widespread application in both indoor and outdoor illumination [17]. As of right now, light-conversion materials for devices include quantum dots and rare-earth-based phosphors [18–20]; however, the application of these materials in LEDs is limited because of their high cost, toxic nature, and poor optical properties [21]. As a result, the development of rare-earth free materials is crucial. There have been reports of non-metal element doping (nitrogen and sulphur) in CQDs [22,23], however research on metal doping in CQDs, is still in its early stages. Doping, meantime, is another well-liked method for boosting the quantum yield and application potential of CQDs [24,25]. Eco-friendly transition metal ions doped CQDs especially Mn and Cu are a good option for use in white LEDs because of their benefits like inexpensive raw materials, ease of acquisition, and superior photoluminescence capabilities [26,27]. Although few published researches have been concentrated on rare earth free white emissive CQDs yet [28,29], it is important to note that it is still urgent and very challenging to prepare high quality white emissive CQDs to meet the needs for a variety of applications in the near future [30].

Introducing two or more applications from a single CQDs boosts effectiveness and significance. In the last years, instrument-based assay of the analyte has become a common technique in a variety of fields including the pharmaceutical sector, heavy hazardous metal ions in medical science, and environmental detections [31]. But the need for an expensive equipment, a skilled operator, and the complexity of sample preparation made it unsuitable for small-scale industries level. The fabrication of PL probes are a good replacement for these instruments and has potential relevance due to their straightforward, cost-effective, and highly accurate manufacturing process [32]. In spite of the fact that metal ions are necessary for life and the environment, but their presence in large quantities are dangerous to all living things including people [31,32], hence, it's crucial to detect metal ions. The selective determination of Fe3+ is particularly crucial as it is one among the essential components for all living beings and biological processes [33]. Similarly, AA, a vital vitamin, functions as an antioxidant, a cofactor in critical enzymatic events, and a free radical scavenger in gene expression, cell division, and a number of other biological processes [34,35]. AA is well-known as a treatment for atherosclerosis, scurvy, liver illness, allergic reactions, drug poisoning, and diabetes [35,36]. It is quite challenging and consequently highly important for human health to diagnose and detect AA in medical tests because of its cross-sensitivity with dopamine and uric acid [34]. To the best of our knowledge, the use of longer wavelength emissive Cu and Mn co-doped CQDs in a simple, cheap, alternative and compatible way as a fluorescent sensor for selective Fe3+ detection and subsequent AA sensing has not been documented before.

In this study, a simple and one-step hydrothermal approach for synthesizing multifunctional CQDs using thio-urea and citric acid as precursors, with reasonable water solubility and exceptional selectivity was developed. It is interesting to note that simultaneous introduction of Cu and Mn on to CQDs causes multicolor solid-state fluorescence and exhibited much enhanced quantum yield (28.35 %). The use of doped CQDs for the purpose of detecting Fe3+ and AA is accomplished via static quenching, and this sensing system could further be used to detect Fe3+ and AA in real water samples. In addition, Cu/Mn:CQDs have outstanding color properties with high CRI value 89, CIE coordinates (0.36, 0.30), and a CCT value of 3923 K meeting the needs of indoor lighting. We suggest that this method of making solid-state luminous materials opens up the possibility of using as-produced doped CQDs in variety of application possibilities not only in sensing but also in white LEDs.

Citric acid (C6H8O7, 99.0 %) and thiourea (CH4N2S, 99.0 %) were obtained from Alfa Aesar. Cu(NO3)2 . 10 H2O (99.99 %) and MnCl2 . 4 H2O, (99.0 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Other analytically pure reagents, not specifically included here, were purchased from commercial sources and utilized precisely as received. Ultrapure water was used in every synthesis and investigations.

Fluorescence spectra were collected on the Agilent Cary Eclipse Spectrophotometer. UV-Vis spectra were captured using Lcd LMSP-UV1900S double beam. The measurements of time resolved photoluminescence were taken using a Time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) apparatus, model FL920 from Edinburgh Instruments in the United Kingdom. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) pictures were produced using a Talos F200X G2 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Perkin Elmer Spectrum100 FTIR was used to record Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) Spectra. X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy was performed using Versa Probe III (XPS) Surface Analysis Instrument. A Nano-ZS90 instrument was used to measure the zeta potential (Malvern Zetasizer, Britain).

Pure CQDs were synthesized using citric acid and thiourea as precursors. 0.6 g of citric acid and 0.125 g of thiourea were dissolved in 20 ml of ultrapure water and pH 8 was maintained using NaOH solution (0.5 N). Afterwards, solution was stirred vigorously for 10 min followed by transfer of solution into Teflon lined stainless steel reactor. Subsequently, sealed reactor was hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 10 hrs. After the reaction completion, the reactor was naturally cooled down to ambient temperature followed by filtration of solution through 0.22 µm syringe filter. The obtained solution was mixed with ultrapure water for characterization purpose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of copper and manganese co-doped CQDs (Cu/Mn:CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize Cu/Mn:CQDs, 12 mg of MnCl2 . 4 H2O and 15 mg of Cu(NO3)2 . 10 H2O were diluted with 5 ml of CQDs solution. The pH of the solution was maintained at 7 by mixing with NaOH solution (0.1 N). After that, the resulting solution was mixed with 1 mg of tartaric acid in order to avoid any coagulation which was stirred for 3 hrs at 100 °C followed by cooling down to room temperature. Finally the as-prepared Cu/Mn:CQDs was stored for various characterizations.

Rhodamine B (Quantum yield= 31 % in water) was used as a reference to determine the QY of the CQDs. The following Eq. (1) was used to determine the QY of CQDs in water [2]:
(1)
Q =
Q

In this equation, Q and Q′ are defined as QY of CQDs and rhodamine B; I and I′ refer to integrated area of fluorescence emission peak (at a specified wavelength) of CQDs and rhodamine B; A and A′ refer to absorbance of CQDs and rhodamine B measured at 340 nm; η (CQDs) and η′ (rhodamine B) stands for the refractive indices of the solvent.

In a typical assay, varying amounts of Fe3+ (0–200 μM) were added to 3 ml of Na-Ac buffer solution (0.2 M, pH=7) containing 50 μL of Cu/Mn:CQDs. The fluorescence emission spectra were then taken after the as-prepared solution had been incubated for 3 min. Additionally, under the same experimental circumstances, Fe3+ was substituted with other metal ions with a final concentration of 200 μM in order to assess the selectivity of the suggested sensors. Three separate replications of each experiment were performed.

In a typical AA detection analysis, different AA concentrations ranging from 0 to 150 μM were gradually added to a Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ solution. The fluorescence emission spectra were then taken after the as-prepared solution had been incubated for 3 min. Next, the selectivity of the Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ based sensor for AA was examined by adding other chemicals in a similar process.

To ascertain whether the sensing system for Fe3+ and AA in challenging real-world samples is feasible, tap water from the laboratory and drinking water from a supermarket in Delhi (India) were chosen as authentic samples for testing the actual performance of the proposed sensor. Both samples of water were filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove contaminants. The samples were then integrated with the sensing platform and FL spectra were recorded after various amounts of Fe3+ and AA were introduced into them.

The typical HRTEM images in Fig. 1 confirm the formation of small nanoparticles having size less than 10 nm. The average diameter of the CQDs and Cu/Mn:CQDs are calculated to be 6.10 and 5.15 nm, respectively as shown in size distribution histogram (Fig. 1) which was extracted from TEM micrograph. Due to introduction of Cu and Mn in CQDs, size is found to be slightly decreased without any effect on the shape of grains. The slight decrease in size of CQDs may be linked to the hindrance to the growth of carbon core by the Cu and Mn doping and promote the production of small CQDs [37]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns embedded in figures show broad circular ring which correspond to (100) plane of graphite and hence confirms amorphous nature of as-synthesized CQDs. Fig. 2 displays the findings of the analysis of the chemical states and surface components of CQDs using XPS spectroscopy. From the survey spectra (Fig. 2a), as-prepared CQDs constitute of elements C (1 s at 285.45 eV), N (1 s at 399.44 eV), O (1 s at 531.60 eV), Mn (2 P at 642.48 eV) and Cu (2 P at 978.24 eV). The C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2b) are deconvoluted into three distinct peaks at 284.37, 285.82 and 287.90 which are assigned to CC/C-C, C-N and CO respectively [38]. According to N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c), the peaks at 399.49 eV and 403.05 eV confirm the presence of C-N/CN and N-H respectively, whereas the deconvoluted O 1 s peaks (Fig. 2d) at 530.97 eV, 532.43 eV and 535.41 eV are attributed to CO, C-OH/C-O-C and C-O, respectively [37]. Also, high resolution Mn 2 P spectrum (Fig. 2e) reveals peaks centered at 640.80 eV and 653.11 eV which are assigned to Mn 2 P3/2 and Mn 2 P1/2, respectively, showing the dominance of Mn4+ [39]. However, a weak peak at around 643.98 eV corresponds to the presence of Mn in + 3 oxidation state [39]. Fig. 2f shows deconvoluted spectrum of Cu 2 P which comprises of two peaks centered at 932.43 eV for Cu 2 P3/2 and 952.41 eV for Cu 2 P1/2, signifying that Cu exists in the form of + 1 oxidation state [40]. Additionally, the satellite peaks are situated at 946.7 eV. These results imply that the sample included copper in the forms of + 1 and + 2 oxidation states [40]. Furthermore, FTIR spectra are used to identify the surface functional groups of as-prepared CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3a, broad absorption peak at 3322 cm−1 is the result of stretching vibration of O-H [41]. The absorption peak at 1633 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CO [42] and another peak appears at 1430 cm−1 which corresponds to the stretching vibrations of C-N [42]. The absorption peak at 945 cm−1 is the result of stretching vibrations of C-O [41]. The FTIR spectra of doped CQDs show additional two peaks centered at 664 and 513 cm−1 which corresponds to Mn-O and Cu-O stretching vibrations [41,43] and also slight shift in the transmittance peak is observed towards longer wave number region hence the incorporation of Cu and Mn in CQDs is confirmed. Thus XPS and FTIR spectra confirm the formation of Cu and Mn doped CQDs.

The UV-Vis absorption spectra of as-synthesized CQDs are presented in Fig. 3b. The absorption peak centered at 227 nm might be assigned to the π-π * transition of CC in the sp2 core. A wide and strong absorption peak centered at 336 nm, attributed to n-π * transitions of surface functional groups (C-N, CO etc.) is observed in CQDs [44]. Furthermore, the absorption edge position is red shifted which is attributed to the in-corporation of Cu and Mn in CQDs [19]. This alters the band gap energy indicating a direct energy transfer between the excited states of CQDs and the 3d levels of the Cu and Mn ions, which are connected through an energy transfer procedure [45,46].

Fig. 3c shows photoluminescence spectra of CQDs at various excitations (280–380 nm). By raising excitation wavelength, the intensity of emission spectra increases at an excitation from 280 to 300 nm and after that the emission presents a redshift with declining intensity at an excitation from 320 to 380 nm. These findings exemplify the excitation dependent nature of CQDs. In Cu/Mn:CQDs (Fig. 3d), due to association of Cu and Mn with surface passivating groups of CQDs via oxygen atoms, extra peaks occur at 577 and 633 nm, along with the emission peak of pure CQDs. It is clear that the dopants Cu ions and Mn ions are responsible for the two emission peaks centered at 577 and 633 nm, respectively. The emission at 577 nm results from the energy transition of excited electrons in CQDs defect states to the 2 T2 state of copper, whereas the emission at 633 nm is attributed to the energy transition of an excited state of 4 T1 to the ground state of 6A1 of Mn [47]. As a result, multicolor luminescence may be achieved by simply tuning the excitation wavelength and it will be more feasible to use only one Cu/Mn:CQDs for practical applications. The fluorescence of doped CQDs is directly related to surface states which includes surface functional groups and degree of surface oxidation [48]. The defects can be caused by surface oxidation of oxygen related groups. Different defects can result in different emission on doped CQDs surface and hence enhancing its fluorescence. The more surface defects implies more excitons and as a result red shift emission is caused by the recombination of trapped excitons [49]. Various transitions of Cu/Mn:CQDs result in different emission, the excited electrons from n-π * transition of CO undergo radiative recombination which give rise to blue emission in Cu/Mn:CQDs [50]. Various epoxide (or hydroxy) related localized electronic states emerge below the π * state as the number of oxygen containing groups on sp2 hybridized carbon increases, resulting in new energy levels between n-π * gaps [50]. Thus the increase of oxygen content on doped CQDs surface results in reduction of band gap i.e; increase of the degree of surface oxidation leads to fluorescence red shift. Plentiful functional groups (such as C-OH, CO, Mn-O, Cu-O, CN) are on the surface of doped CQDs as oxygen/nitrogen related functional groups provide tunable multicolor luminescence which introduce two new energy levels (HOMO nN and HOMO no) and thus new electronic transitions are produced as shown in Fig. 4a [51,52]. As a result when electrons in O/N related defect states return to HOMO, red shift will occur. Furthermore, white fluorescence is caused by the overlap of various emissions due to electron transitions between energy levels [51]. Also, the computed PLQY of pure CQDs and doped CQDs are 11.17 % and 28.35 %, respectively, indicating that Cu/Mn:CQDs have a higher PLQY than pure CQDs. The presence of impurity atoms introduces defect sites, altering the energy states and adding new paths for electron transition in the band structure of Cu/Mn:CQDs, leading to a markedly higher fluorescence quantum yield [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-color emission for solid-state LED application
ce:section.ce:para:
As a consequence of color-tunable PL properties of CQDs by the introduction of Cu and Mn dopants, we have achieved multi-color phosphor for LEDs with decent performance by increasing the excitation wavelength from 280 to 380 nm as shown in Commission Internationale de L′Eclairage (CIE) diagram (Fig. 4b). As the excitation wavelength is increased in step of 20 nm from 280 to 380 nm, the phosphor show CIE color-coordinates of (0.24, 0.23), (0.36, 0.30), (0.39, 0.27), (0.43, 0.28), (0.48, 0.31) and (0.54, 0.36), respectively and the white color phosphor exhibit robust color rendering index of 89 as shown in Table 1. What’s more, it is worth introducing that CCT values of these phosphors are determined by employing the McCamy empirical formula (2) [54]:
(2)
CCT = − 473
n
3
+ 3601
n
2
− 6861 n + 5514.31
where, n = (x-xe)/(y-ye) and xe = 0.3320 and ye = 0.1858 are the locations of chromaticity epicentres. The estimated CCT value of white color phosphor are 3923 K which validate its application in warm LEDs and hence are suitable for indoor lightning application [55]. Furthermore, compared to previous reports of CQDs based phosphor for LEDs [56–59] as shown in Table 2, the luminous efficiency is relatively low but this work suggests a novel study to concurrently produce an effective multi-color phosphor having solid-state long wavelength emission characteristics. Thus the strategy for preparing white light emissive material as single phosphor is successfully achieved with enhancement of CRI value up to 89 which overcome the major drawback of high cost lanthanide based phosphor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses towards Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: Since Cu/Mn:CQDs exhibit great intensity, broad emission spectra, and a high PL QY of 28.35 % as revealed by their photoluminescence investigation. Because of the high PL QY, only a tiny percentage of the sample produced a significant level of PL intensity, which highlights fluorescence change more noticeable. Additionally, a broad emission spectrum is expected to increase the detection concentration range. Therefore, Cu/Mn:CQDs is a promising candidate for a fluorescent probe with a greater detection range.

Previous studies have shown that oxygen-containing groups, such as hydroxyl groups, have great binding affinity towards Fe3+ [60]. Due to the presence of multiple oxygen-containing groups on Cu/Mn:CQDs, it is anticipated that it will function as a fluorescent nanosensor for the determination of Fe3+. Fe3+, one of the basic components in the human body, is well-known for playing an indispensable role in blood transport and haematopoiesis [61]. Diseases including metabolic irregularities, cell anaemia, tissue damage, and even organ failure may result from Fe3+ problems in humans [62]. Although many analytical techniques for determining Fe3+ have been successfully established, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [63], electrochemical [64] and chromatography [65], work is still being done to find even more straightforward, efficient, and sensitive ways.

Firstly, investigations on the effect of pH value on the PL performance of the Cu/Mn:CQDs solution were carried out. As can be seen in the Fig. 5a, addition of various pH values (2−11) caused a small variation in fluorescence intensity. Here, F0 represents the intensity of the blank sample and F represents the fluorescence intensity at 645 nm following the incorporation of an outside component which in this case is a variable pH. Hence the current sensor system is little sensitive towards pH and finally we have selected pH 7 for the entire experiment. In addition, fluorescence intensity remains unaltered under high ionic strength. As demonstrated in Fig. 5b, it is seen that fluorescence intensity remains unaffected in presence of ionic NaCl up to concentrations of 2.0 M. According to all of these findings, Cu/Mn:CQDs exhibit relatively strong optical stability under various experimental conditions, which ensures their effective application in biosensing and environmental fields.

Additionally, the correlation between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration was studied. As seen in Fig. 6a, the PL intensity of Cu/Mn:CQDs gradually decreased as the concentrations of Fe3+ are increased. The linear response of Cu/Mn:CQDs towards Fe3+ was seen in the range of 0–80 µM (the inset of Fig. 6a) with the calibration equation of F0/F = 0.0087(Fe3+) + 0.0115 where, F0 and F denote the PL intensities of Cu/Mn:CQDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. With the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 16 nM (using equation s/k = 3, where k implies the slope of the calibration curve ang s implies standard deviation), an excellent linear correlation (R2 = 0.992) was attained. Table 3 displays the achievements of several Fe3+ detection techniques in terms of working range and LOD [66–71]. In comparison to these findings, the suggested sensing platform performs better.

Fluorescence response tests (Fig. 6b) of different ions (Na+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Ca2+, K+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Co2+, Ba2+) are carried out under the same circumstances to further evaluate the selectivity of Cu/Mn:CQDs for Fe3+. As was pretty evident, the inclusion of Fe3+ significantly reduced the fluorescence of CQDs, while the other additives had little to no effect on it. This result confirms the selectivity of Cu/Mn:CQDs as a "turn-off" fluorescent system towards Fe3+. Notably, Fe2+ among the aforementioned ions did not significantly alter the intensity of the PL. Knowing that the presence of AA can cause Fe3+ to be reduced to Fe2+, it is possible to develop a "turn-on" chemosensor that can detect AA. Because of this, addition of AA allowed the PL quenched Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ probe to be restored. Thus for the purpose of AA detection, the Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ can be utilized as a fluorescent "turn-on" sensing platform.

Using the aforesaid technique, the Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ sensing system's sensitivity and selectivity were assessed. As shown in Fig. 7a, the increasing AA concentration gradually increased the PL intensity of the Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ system. Additionally, a substantial linearity relationship between F0/F and AA concentration (0–80 µM) having correlation coefficient equal to 0.995 was found which can be fitted into F0/F = −0.0220(AA) + 3.7988 where, the PL intensities of Cu/Mn:CQDs and Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ in presence of AA are F0 and F, respectively (the inset of Fig. 7a). The detection limit down to 46 nM is obtained based on signal to noise ratio of 3. To our knowledge, the detection limit and linear range are lower than those that have previously been reported by other fluorescent chemosensors for the detection of AA ( Table 4) [72–76]. As a necessary procedure, the selectivity of Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ towards AA was investigated against a number of chemicals, including glutathione, glucose, citric acid, dopamine, urea, folic acid, and aspartic acid. As is evident from Fig. 7b, when AA and other foreign chemicals were added to the Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ sensor, only AA shows considerable enhancement, demonstrating the effective selectivity of this sensing platform based on the "turn-on" fluorescence principle for AA detection.

As evidenced by XPS and FTIR, numerous oxygen-containing functional groups with strong affinities towards Fe3+ are found. It is observed that Fe3+ ion exhibits significant fluorescence quenching in the as-synthesized Cu/Mn:CQDs due to the strong chelation efficiency or coordination contacts between them [44]. During this process, a portion of the electrons and charges in the excited state of CQDs were made to pass on the partially filled 3d of Fe3+, which results in quenching of PL [62]. After the addition of Fe3+, the zeta potential of Cu/Mn:CQDs changed from − 6.76 mV to 2.18 mV ( Fig. 8a), further confirming the co-ordination action between Fe3+ ion and the organic functional groups on the surface of CQDs [58]. In addition, Cu/Mn:CQDs and Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ are subjected to a fluorescence lifetime decay test as shown in Fig. 8b which reveals that fast exciton recombination phenomena are observed having average life time of 1.27 ns as obtained from decay analysis. Additionally, the formation of non-fluorescence complex between CQDs as a result of chelation with Fe3+ is observed which exhibits a static quenching as the average life time show almost negligible change being ∼ 1.28 ns [77]. After that, the PL intensity of the CQDs gets restored by the addition of AA, which is probably because of the redox reaction between the Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ and AA. Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+ by AA in case of Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ system and hence non-radiative electron transport is minimized and the PL of the Cu/Mn:CQDs is restored [61].

Using the conventional addition process, the prepared doped CQDs are used to detect Fe3+ in tap and drinking water in order to assess the viability of this method. Before analysis, different concentration of Fe3+ are added to tap and drinking water. With a relative standard deviation (RSD) around 2.21 %, the sensing platform having good recoveries for each Fe3+ concentration are seen in the range of 96.6–104.1 % ( Table 5). These findings indicate that this sensor is remarkably practical for monitoring Fe3+. Additionally, the identical tests were carried out with Cu/Mn:CQDs/Fe3+ system in tap and drinking water. The acquired results which are presented in Table 5, showed that recovery values were in the range of 98.9–106.6 % with an RSD of less than 4.68 %, demonstrating its superior capability of detecting AA in real samples. Thus the aforementioned findings show that the suggested sensing platform is reliable and practical for determining Fe3+ and AA in real water samples.

The fluorescence switching characteristic of Cu/Mn:CQDs can be exploited to produce multiple molecular logic gates that perform Boolean algebraic logic operations when Fe3+ and AA are present [78]. By employing Fe3+ alone as an input signal, the straightforward one input molecular logic gate NOT is built as shown in Fig. 9a. Also, an IMPLICATION molecular logic gate is developed by using Fe3+ and AA as inputs. When Fe3+ or AA are present, their presence and absence is counted as "1" and "0", respectively. For output, we define the strong fluorescence as "1" and the corresponding weak fluorescence as "0". As seen from Fig. 9b, the resulting output is 0 in the presence of Fe3+ and absence of AA (1/0). Conversely, in case of other inputs (0/0, 0/1, 1/1), the gate (output) are turned on and it provide the output signal "1". Fig. 9 displays the output fluorescence intensity, the truth table, and the logic symbol.

This study suggests a simple and effective method for producing high-performance CQDs based phosphor by the introduction of Cu2+ and Mn2+ dopants with multicolor fluorescence emission. The fabricated CQDs based phosphor exhibits exceptionally strong white light emission at 300 nm excitation wavelength, with chromatic color coordinates of (0.36, 0.30) and a CRI of 89, which is a high value recorded for a single system white light producing Cu/Mn:CQDs based phosphor supporting quality of white light generated. Additionally, using a fluorescence off-on switch strategy, Cu/Mn:CQDs were effectively used as a highly sensitive and selective chemosensor for the detection of Fe3+ and AA and an "IMPLICATION" logic gate based on the novel Cu/Mn:CQDs has been constructed in accordance with this phenomena. More significantly, the fabricated fluorescent dual-functional sensing system can be utilized to monitor Fe3+ and AA in real samples with high recovery. The investigations provide a simple assay for the efficient, large-scale synthesis of Cu/Mn:CQDs-based phosphors, which holds considerable promise for use in a variety of fields, including lighting and sensing technology.

Urosa Latief: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Shafi UL Islam: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mohd. Shahid Khan: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.
